translation: By those [winds] sent forth in gusts
commentary:
**وَٱلْمُرْسَلَـٰتِ عُرْفًا**

The surah opens with a powerful oath, and surahs that begin with oaths require deep contemplation to understand properly. The word المُرْسَلَاتِ comes from the root أَرْسَلَ, meaning "to send" or "to release." The feminine plural form مُرْسَلَات is used here because it describes non-human entities. The most convincing interpretation is that this refers to the winds that Allah ﷻ has released.

The word عُرْفًا originally describes the mane of a horse—specifically, the hair on the back of a horse's neck. With a domesticated horse, you control it using reins, a saddle, and a rope. But with a wild horse, if you want to pull it back and slow it down, the only thing you can grab is the mane. The imagery here is striking: these winds are being compared to wild horses whose manes have been released. When someone holds the mane tightly, the horse remains calm, producing a gentle breeze. But when Allah ﷻ lets go of those reins and releases the mane, these winds transform into something powerful and uncontrollable.

translation: And the winds that blow violently
commentary:
**فَٱلْعَـٰصِفَـٰتِ عَصْفًا**

The particle فَ at the beginning connects this ayah to the previous one, showing cause and effect: once the manes are released, the winds become الْعَاصِفَاتِ—violently storming winds. The word عَصْف describes ruthless, indiscriminate motion. It's movement that doesn't care what stands in its path. A bulldozer or tractor plowing through terrain exemplifies this quality—it doesn't pause or divert for obstacles.

These winds, once released, tremble and trample over everything. They crush through and plow through anything that comes in their path relentlessly. What we're learning here is that Allah ﷻ controls the gentle breeze we feel every day. If He decides one day to release those reins, those same winds become forces of indiscriminate destruction—tornadoes, hurricanes, and devastating storms.

translation: And [by] the winds that spread [clouds]
commentary:
**وَٱلنَّـٰشِرَٰتِ نَشْرًا**

The particle وَ here indicates a separate oath, introducing another aspect of the winds. The word نَاشِرَات comes from نَشْر, which means to distribute or scatter. Picture someone taking a handful of seeds and throwing them on the ground—the seeds scatter in every direction. This is the imagery of nashr.

Allah ﷻ swears by the winds that distribute and scatter. But what do these winds distribute? They carry pollen from plant to plant. They move clouds across the sky, bringing rain to different regions. They transport seeds that will grow into vegetation. The very life and greenery of the earth depends on this distribution system. If the wind ceased to blow, clouds wouldn't move. If clouds didn't move, certain parts of the world would experience complete drought. The winds are essential delivery mechanisms for life on earth.

translation: And those [angels] who bring criterion
commentary:
**فَٱلْفَـٰرِقَـٰتِ فَرْقًا**

Here again, the فَ shows consequence: as a result of this distribution, the winds are also الْفَارِقَات—those that separate and divide decisively. This might seem contradictory at first. When you throw seeds on the ground, they scatter randomly in every direction—you have no control over which seed goes where. Yet these winds are described as very decisive and precise in their division.

Allah ﷻ has determined exactly where each wind will carry its cargo. These winds decide—by Allah's command—where rain will fall, where pollen will drop, which plant will be pollinated. Every drop of rain, every grain of pollen has a predetermined destination. The winds are extremely divided and specific in where they go, implying very precise motion in every bit of this movement.

Notice what we've learned so far: in one set of oaths, winds are a means by which death and destruction come about. In the other set, winds are a means by which life flourishes. Yet at the end of the day, it's the same creation—the winds. The same force that can destroy can also give life.

translation: And those [angels] who deliver a message
commentary:
**فَٱلْمُلْقِيَـٰتِ ذِكْرًا**

All of these winds—both the winds of destruction and the winds of life—constantly bring people into contact with ذِكْر, or reminder. The word مُلْقِيَات comes from أَلْقَى, meaning to throw or to bring into contact. These winds are continuously throwing reminders at humanity, constantly slapping us with messages that should awaken our consciousness.

What reminder comes from the wind? First, there's the warning: if Allah ﷻ simply releases the reins, if He lets go of the manes, what will happen? Destruction would be immediate and total. Second, there's the blessing: all the life we see around us—the trees, the crops, the flowers—is being delivered to us by the beautiful breeze we feel on our faces. That gentle wind on your skin is delivering life to the earth.

Consider this example: imagine a rebellious dog that bites and barks at its master. If that master cruelly decided to cage the dog and leave it in the sun without food or water for two days, that dog would be dying of dehydration and starvation, tongue hanging out, barely alive. Two days earlier, if you brought your hand near that dog, it would bite. But now, dying of thirst, when you bring water in your hand near the dog, will it bite? No. It will quietly drink. All the rebellion, all the rowdiness, all the craziness would be out of its system.

If Allah ﷻ wanted to end the rebellion of humanity—if He wanted to humble us like rowdy dogs—what would He need to do? Simply stop the winds. All of our arrogance would disappear from our system. Everyone would be praying desperately for rain. We would be humbled like that dog.

We are reminded constantly of how all these creations of Allah—the trees, the sky, the earth, the clouds—work in cooperation with each other so we can drink and eat. What kind of Master is this, who controls all of these intricate systems so we can have a simple glass of water? And the same thing that is a blessing can be turned by Allah ﷻ into a curse. The same winds that bring life can bring death.

translation: As justification or warning
commentary:
**عُذْرًا أَوْ نُذْرًا**

This ayah is a powerful concluding statement to the oaths. The winds serve as عُذْر—an excuse or justification from Allah ﷻ—or as نُذْر—a warning. This is an example of eloquent brevity in the Quran.

The word عُذْر means justification or excuse. When people stand before Allah ﷻ on Judgment Day and say, "I didn't receive any revelation. I didn't understand Your message. I didn't know what You wanted from me," Allah ﷻ has the right to respond: "Didn't you feel the wind?" That alone is sufficient. Allah's justification is already established. His excuse to hold us accountable is complete—we have no excuse remaining because we felt the wind. The wind was enough to reflect upon, enough to wake us up and get our act together.

On the other hand, for someone who does respond and get his act together, winds alone are enough as نُذْر—a warning. The wind by itself is sufficient warning of Allah's power.

What the Quran accomplishes in these ayahs is one of its miracles: a believer in the Quran can no longer look at nature the way anyone else looks at nature. The wind becomes a sermon. The breeze becomes a reminder that Allah ﷻ has the power to destroy. It's a complete change of perspective.

This is why our religion is fundamentally about attitude—it's a way of looking at things. Islam changes the way we look at nature, the way we look at each other, the way we look at society, history, and the future. Everything is seen through the lens of the Quran. When someone internalizes this, they develop a different personality entirely.

But just because we're going through this study right now doesn't mean every time you feel the wind, you'll automatically think these thoughts. That's why reminder is necessary. The Quran reinforces this way of thinking through constant reminder. Every time the Quran makes reference to things in creation, it's supposed to bring you back to something in revelation. Revelation makes you think about creation. Creation is supposed to make you think about revelation.

Revelation is called آيَات—signs. Creation is also called آيَات. The ayat in the Book remind you of the ayat in creation. The ayat in creation remind you of the ayat in revelation. You're constantly surrounded by ayat—by reminders. There's no excuse. No one can say, if they truly understand this, that there are no reminders around them. We are surrounded by reminders. There's no shortage of them.

translation: Indeed, what you are promised is to occur
commentary:
**إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَوَٰقِعٌ**

The Quraysh would say, "We have enough power among ourselves. We don't need to be intimidated by you—a handful of slaves, foreigners, and young people. You're just a small, pathetic group. What are you going to do? What army will you bring?"

Allah ﷻ responds: He doesn't need to amass an army, pass resolutions, and send forces across the desert to attack you. He can simply release the winds. That army is already slapping you in the face every day. It's just that Allah ﷻ hasn't commanded it yet. Know your place.

إِنَّمَا is a particle that emphasizes exclusivity and certainty. Whatever you have been promised—the Day of Judgment, Hellfire, the destruction of nations, the power of Allah ﷻ, the coming of resurrection, the questioning and interrogation—all of these promises are summed up in one statement: لَوَاقِعٌ—it is certainly going to occur. It's going to drop. It's going to happen. There is no doubt about it.

If you've reflected enough on the winds, then the conclusion you should reach is absolute certainty: whatever Allah ﷻ has promised is bound to happen.

translation: So when the stars are obliterated
commentary:
**فَإِذَا ٱلنُّجُومُ طُمِسَتْ**

Once that promise does happen, when the stars are طُمِسَتْ—completely erased. The word طَمَسَ means to erase so thoroughly that you cannot even find traces of what was there. The stars will be obliterated, disappeared, utterly gone from the sky.

translation: And when the heaven is opened
commentary:
**وَإِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ فُرِجَتْ**

And when the sky is فُرِجَتْ—torn open. This comes from فَرَجَ, meaning to rip or tear apart violently. The sky itself will be ripped open like fabric being torn.

translation: And when the mountains are blown away
commentary:
**وَإِذَا ٱلْجِبَالُ نُسِفَتْ**

And when the mountains are نُسِفَتْ—plucked out. The word نَسَفَ literally means to pluck, like pulling a weak plant out of the ground with its roots sticking out at the bottom. Think of how farmers pull carrots or potatoes from the ground. Allah ﷻ describes that this is what He will do with mountains. Mountains—these massive, seemingly permanent features of the earth—will be plucked out of the ground like vegetables.

translation: And when the messengers' time has come
commentary:
**وَإِذَا ٱلرُّسُلُ أُقِّتَتْ**

And when the messengers are أُقِّتَتْ. This word comes from the root وَقَتَ. which means to set a specific time for something, to schedule something, to mark a deadline. The intensive form أُقِّتَتْ implies setting something firmly in stone, etching it permanently into the calendar—not with dry-erase marker, but permanently marked.

When the messengers are given their final appointment, when the deadline for them has arrived. This could mean the messengers of Allah ﷻ who are being brought as witnesses against their nations. Your time has come—give your testimony. Like witnesses called to testify in court, they're given a date and time to appear, stand trial, and give their testimony.

Alternatively, this could refer to the angels who have been assigned to deliver destruction to humanity on Judgment Day. They receive their orders: the deadline is here, the time has come. Execute the command.

translation: For what Day was it postponed
commentary:
**لِأَىِّ يَوْمٍ أُجِّلَتْ**

For what day have all of them been postponed? The word أُجِّلَتْ comes from أَجَّلَ, meaning to postpone, to delay, to put things off. Why hasn't Judgment Day come yet? Why isn't it happening right now, or tomorrow? Why has it been given so much time? Why is it delayed?

translation: For the Day of Judgement
commentary:
**لِيَوْمِ ٱلْفَصْلِ**

For يَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ—the Day of Final Separation and Decision. Allah ﷻ is saying: right now, My purpose is to give you more time, to give you ease, to keep the winds held back, to not destroy nations, so that you can get your act together. That's why it hasn't happened yet. The delay itself is a mercy.

The word فَصْل literally means separation or division. It comes from the same root as تَفْسِير, which means explanation or interpretation. A good teacher takes complex, intertwined ideas and separates them: "Let's understand part one, then part two, then part three, and then we'll put it all together." That's tafsir—breaking things apart and explaining them clearly.

فَصْل also means decisive—a final verdict, a done deal with no ifs, ands, or buts. There are no reconsiderations, no appeals after that. In worldly courts, when a judgment is given, you can appeal it, apply to another court, counter the judgment, request a mistrial. None of those options will exist on يَوْمِ الْفَصْلِ. It is the day of final, irreversible decision.

translation: And what can make you know what is the Day of Judgement
commentary:
**وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ**

And what could give you the slightest clue what the Day of Final Separation is? This rhetorical question emphasizes the incomprehensible magnitude of that Day. No human experience, no analogy, no description can truly convey what يَوْمُ الْفَصْلِ will be like.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

وَيْلٌ—terrible destruction, utter ruin—on that Day will be for الْمُكَذِّبِينَ—those who persistently denied and considered it all a lie. Those who rejected Islam, the Quran, the Prophet ﷺ, Judgment Day, and the message—disaster awaits them.

translation: Did We not destroy the former peoples
commentary:
**أَلَمْ نُهْلِكِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ**

Didn't We destroy الْأَوَّلِينَ—the earliest ones, the ancient nations? This is a rhetorical question directed at the Arabs, particularly the Quraysh. Don't you learn from history? The earliest civilizations that rebelled and disbelieved—weren't they annihilated?

translation: Then We will follow them with the later ones
commentary:
**ثُمَّ نُتْبِعُهُمُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ**

Then We sent الْآخِرِينَ—the later ones, the more recent nations—right behind them to the same fate. When they followed suit, when they disobeyed, disbelieved, and ridiculed the message, We annihilated them too. Nation after nation, the pattern repeats. Don't you Arabs of Makkah realize this?

translation: Thus do We deal with the criminals
commentary:
**كَذَٰلِكَ نَفْعَلُ بِٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ**

كَذَٰلِكَ—just like that, in exactly that way—نَفْعَلُ بِالْمُجْرِمِينَ—that's how We deal with criminals. This is the formula. It hasn't changed and it won't change. This is how Allah ﷻ deals with those who commit crimes against truth and justice.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

Again: terrible destruction on that Day for those who continuously lie and deny. The repetition emphasizes the stubbornness of the deniers. They need to hear it over and over again until it penetrates their consciousness and gets into their heads.

translation: Did We not create you from a liquid disdained
commentary:
**أَلَمْ نَخْلُقكُّم مِّن مَّآءٍ مَّهِينٍ**

Didn't We create you from مَاءٍ مَّهِينٍ—humiliating water? The word مَهِين comes from هَانَ, meaning humiliation or lowliness. This is the third time in recent surahs that Allah ﷻ has made reference to the humbling origin of human creation. Over and over again, the reminder comes: Don't you remember where you came from?

translation: And We placed it in a firm lodging
commentary:
**فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ فِى قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ**

Then We placed it in قَرَارٍ مَّكِينٍ—a secure, established resting place. The word قَرَار indicates a place where something settles and doesn't move. مَكِين means secure, protected, safe. This refers to the womb of the mother, where the developing child remains stationary and protected.

translation: For a known extent
commentary:
**إِلَىٰ قَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ**

Until قَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ—a known measure or appointed time. The calculation is known—but known to whom? Known to Allah ﷻ. He alone knows the precise timing of development and birth.

translation: And We determined [it], and excellent [are We] to determine
commentary:
**فَقَدَرْنَا فَنِعْمَ ٱلْقَـٰدِرُونَ**

Then We measured and calculated it precisely. فَنِعْمَ الْقَادِرُونَ—and how excellent, how awesome We are at calculating! How will the fetus grow? How will the baby develop? When will the arms form? When will you be able to see the features? When will the eyes develop? When will the baby be old enough to be delivered? Will it be delivered two months early? Two weeks late? Right on schedule?

The doctor gives you a delivery date, but it's never exactly that—it's always somewhere around that time. The precision of fetal development, the intricate calculations governing every stage of growth, demonstrate Allah's perfect knowledge and power. He is the best of those who measure and decree.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

Terrible destruction on that Day will fall upon those who lie against the truth. This refrain echoes throughout the Surah, reminding us that denial carries consequences. The word وَيْلٌ speaks to devastation that awaits those who rejected the message.

translation: Have We not made the earth a container
commentary:
**أَلَمْ نَجْعَلِ ٱلْأَرْضَ كِفَاتًا**

Didn't We make the earth كِفَاتًا - a container? The earth functions as a bowl, holding everything that lives upon its surface. Allah ﷻ mentions first the sky and then the earth repeatedly throughout the Quran. After discussing the heavens, He brings our attention to the earth beneath our feet - the next room after examining what's above. This is why the phrase "Mother Earth" resonates across cultures. The earth carries us, nurtures us, sustains us.

translation: Of the living and the dead
commentary:
**أَحْيَآءً وَأَمْوَٰتًا**

Living and dead. The earth contains both the living who walk upon it and the dead who rest within it. It comes to life with vegetation and dies in drought, cycling through seasons of vitality and dormancy.

translation: And We placed therein lofty, firmly set mountains and have given you to drink sweet water
commentary:
**وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ شَـٰمِخَـٰتٍ وَأَسْقَيْنَـٰكُم مَّآءً فُرَاتًا**

We placed in it رَوَٰسِىَ شَـٰمِخَـٰتٍ - high, towering mountain ranges. These aren't just hills but massive geological formations that stand tall and firm. And We gave you to drink مَّآءً فُرَاتًا - water that is sweet and fresh. These signs of Allah's ﷻ power and mercy surround us constantly, yet people deny the Day of Judgment.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

What terrible destruction on that Day will fall upon those who considered all of this a lie.

translation: [They will be told], "Proceed to that which you used to deny
commentary:
**ٱنطَلِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ**

The command comes: March forward! Move toward the very thing you used to lie against. You denied it all this time, claiming there was no destruction coming. Now go see it for yourselves. The previous Surah already established the imagery - the chains, the fetters, the criminals bound. Now those criminals are being transported to their destination.

translation: Proceed to a shadow [of smoke] having three columns
commentary:
**ٱنطَلِقُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ ظِلٍّ ذِى ثَلَـٰثِ شُعَبٍ**

Move toward ظِلٍّ ذِى ثَلَـٰثِ شُعَبٍ - a gigantic shade with three branches. The Hellfire has smoke billowing from it in three directions: forward, left and right. Imagine three massive towers of smoke, and behind you are the guardian angels pushing you forward. There's no going back - you can only move forward, right or left, and all three directions lead to the same fate.

translation: [But having] no cool shade and availing not against the flame
commentary:
**لَّا ظَلِيلٍ وَلَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهَبِ**

But there's no actual shade there. The word ظَلِيلٍ refers to cool, comfortable shade that protects you from heat. The "shade" mentioned earlier is actually burning smoke. It doesn't save you from اللَّهَبِ - the burning, red fire. It provides no relief, no protection, no comfort.

translation: Indeed, it throws sparks [as huge] as a fortress
commentary:
**إِنَّهَا تَرْمِى بِشَرَرٍ كَٱلْقَصْرِ**

Then the smoke itself starts shooting بِشَرَرٍ - balls of fire - كَٱلْقَصْرِ, as large as domes. The word qasr refers to the dome atop a castle, that shining part visible from a distance. From far away, you'll see these enormous fireballs being launched from those three towers of smoke.

translation: As if they were yellowish [black] camels
commentary:
**كَأَنَّهُۥ جِمَـٰلَتٌ صُفْرٌ**

When they land, they'll look like جِمَـٰلَتٌ صُفْرٌ - rows of yellow camels. Picture how flames appear yellow and red - there will be this vivid image of yellow and red fire against dark black smoke.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

What terrible destruction on that Day falls upon those who considered the truth - Islam, the Prophet ﷺ, the Quran - all lies. Notice Allah ﷻ isn't mentioned by name because the denial of all these things ultimately represents denial of Him.

translation: This is a Day they will not speak
commentary:
**هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ لَا يَنطِقُونَ**

This is the Day لَا يَنطِقُونَ - of no talking for you. You don't get to run your mouth. You don't get to open your mouth. You don't get to utter a word. The word نُطْق means pronunciation, the act of uttering sound. Not a peep comes out of you.

translation: Nor will it be permitted for them to make an excuse
commentary:
**وَلَا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ**

No permission will be given to them فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ - that they may make excuses for themselves. Remember, the previous Surah already addressed this: throw all the excuses you want in this life, but there are no excuses on that Day. No permission will be granted for you to offer any justification.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

What terrible destruction will fall upon those who continuously lied.

translation: This is the Day of Judgement; We will have assembled you and the former peoples
commentary:
**هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ ۖ جَمَعْنَـٰكُمْ وَٱلْأَوَّلِينَ**

This is يَوْمُ ٱلْفَصْلِ - the Day of Separation and Final Decision. We have gathered you and الْأَوَّلِينَ - the earliest nations, the most ancient of peoples. Everyone has been assembled. This time they're not categorized by ethnicity, race or the nationalism they wore on their sleeves. They're simply categorized by destination: who's heading toward Paradise and who's heading toward Fire.

translation: So if you have a plan, then plan against Me
commentary:
**فَإِن كَانَ لَكُمْ كَيْدٌ فَكِيدُونِ**

If you have any kind of كَيْدٌ - plot or scheme - فَكِيدُونِ - then go ahead and plot against Me. Some of the best minds dedicated to hatred against Islam will all be gathered in one place. We have enemies of Islam and enemies of truth in every generation. Let's combine all the enemies of all the generations in one mega-conference on Judgment Day. Now make your plot. Go ahead, plan away. Knock yourselves out. Scheme all you want.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

What terrible destruction is going to fall upon those who considered all of this a lie.

translation: Indeed, the righteous will be among shades and springs
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ وَعُيُونٍ**

No doubt, الْمُتَّقِينَ - those who truly protected themselves in this life through consciousness of Allah ﷻ - will be فِى ظِلَـٰلٍ, in smooth, cool shades, وَعُيُونٍ, in the company of springs of water.

translation: And fruits from whatever they desire
commentary:
**وَفَوَٰكِهَ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ**

And all kinds of fruits مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ - from which they really, really love to eat. There are fruits you like, and then there are fruits you absolutely crave. Imagine a fruit platter where you might leave certain fruits on the side and eat only your favorites first. In Paradise, every fruit you taste will be the one you really, really want at that moment. You'll think, "I can't wait to have some strawberries," and that strawberry will be exactly what you were craving. The word فَاكِهَة itself comes from فَكَّهَ, which means to smile. These are fruits that make you smile - foods that taste so good that even if you're the grumpiest person on earth, one bite forces a smile onto your face. Even in deep sadness, this food brings joy. That's the quality of fruit in Paradise.

translation: [Being told], "Eat and drink in satisfaction for what you used to do
commentary:
**كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ هَنِيٓـًٔۢا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ**

كُلُوا۟ وَٱشْرَبُوا۟ - Eat and drink هَنِيٓـًٔۢا - carefree, without worry. Don't worry about calories. Don't worry about eating too much. There's no feeling of being overstuffed. Just eat, relax, enjoy yourself completely. بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - because of the things you used to do.

One of the greatest gifts in this ayah isn't the food, the drink or even the carefree state. It's the form of address. Notice how Allah ﷻ switches to speaking directly to you - second person. He doesn't say "they will be told" or "it will be said to them." He says directly to us: eat and drink. He forces us to imagine ourselves in Paradise. He makes us visualize the Day when we'll hear these words from Allah ﷻ Himself: "Eat and drink. Don't worry. You did really well."

That last part truly moves the heart. We don't get to Paradise because of our deeds - we get there because of Allah's ﷻ mercy. But here's the magnificence of Allah ﷻ: He gets us into Paradise through His mercy, and then He says, "You did really well." He gives us a special meal and says it's because of what we did. We never get to claim we earned Paradise through our deeds. We must always acknowledge we got there through His mercy. Yet He lets us in and then takes pride in our deeds. He honors the efforts of His slaves. That's the proper relationship: we rely completely on the mercy of our Master, and He honors our efforts. When we reverse that and start relying on our deeds, that's when problems arise. Our deeds alone don't get us Paradise.

translation: Indeed, We thus reward the doers of good
commentary:
**إِنَّا كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ**

Indeed, that is how We compensate الْمُحْسِنِينَ - those who have excelled, the people of excellence, those who embodied ihsan in their lives.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

What terrible destruction, what terrible outcome awaits those on that Day who lied against all of this.

translation: [O disbelievers], eat and enjoy yourselves a little; indeed, you are criminals
commentary:
**كُلُوا۟ وَتَمَتَّعُوا۟ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكُم مُّجْرِمُونَ**

Now Allah ﷻ addresses a different audience. After speaking to the people of Paradise, He turns to the disbelievers: You go ahead - كُلُوا۟, eat, and تَمَتَّعُوا۟, enjoy yourselves. Live it up. Party however you want. قَلِيلًا - but just a little while though. How long are you going to live? Go ahead, do whatever you want, but إِنَّكُم مُّجْرِمُونَ - that doesn't change the fact that you are criminals. No doubt about it. Your temporary pleasures don't erase your criminality.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

How terrible a destruction will fall upon those who lied against this message, who considered it all a lie.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Bow [in prayer]," they do not bow
commentary:
**وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱرْكَعُوا۟ لَا يَرْكَعُونَ**

When it's said to them ارْكَعُوا۟ - bow down, humble yourselves before your Lord - لَا يَرْكَعُونَ, they don't humble themselves. They refuse to put themselves down in submission before Allah ﷻ.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

What terrible destruction falls upon those who consider all of this a lie.

translation: Then in what statement after the Qur'an will they believe
commentary:
**فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ بَعْدَهُۥ يُؤْمِنُونَ**

Then فَبِأَىِّ حَدِيثٍۭ - what speech, what message - بَعْدَهُۥ, after this - يُؤْمِنُونَ, will they believe in? The word حَدِيث means something new, a fresh address, words they haven't heard before. Allah ﷻ delivered the message in a completely new way for this particular audience, this stubborn crowd. After hearing this speech, if they still don't come to faith, what else could possibly convince them?

This declaration specifically addressed those who heard the Quran first. For them, Allah ﷻ sealed the judgment: after this, your case is closed. The Surah is universal for us, but its original context had a particular audience, and that audience received a final verdict. There are still others who have hope, but for those who rejected even this magnificent speech, what new revelation could they possibly believe in?
